segments.title: Danish defences and the failed relief effort
segments.summary: The siege begins with Copenhagen's garrison, fortifications, and Wellesley's defeat of a Danish relief force.
segments.body: Copenhagen had roughly 10,000 defenders, split between regulars and militia, manning a broad fortified perimeter that included a major star fort. The Danish position looked formidable on paper, yet Wellesley's reserve and supporting artillery defeated the force sent to relieve the city. The contrast introduces the episode's central puzzle: why a prepared defence produced so little effective resistance to the British expedition.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Danish garrison, its fortifications, and Wellesley's action against the relief force.

segments.title: The bombardment of Copenhagen
segments.summary: Britain's demand for the Danish fleet was followed by a four-day bombardment that used fire-setting rockets against a wooden city.
segments.body: After demanding the fleet, the British bombarded Copenhagen from 2 to 5 September 1807 when Denmark refused. The account highlights Congreve rockets, whose incendiary effect was especially dangerous among wooden buildings and ships. British permission for civilians to leave reduced the number of people trapped in the city, but it did not prevent widespread material destruction.

evidence.note: The transcript states the British demand, the 2-5 September bombardment, civilian evacuation, and the incendiary use of Congreve rockets.

segments.title: Why did a strong defence fail so quickly?
segments.summary: The video asks why Britain faced so few losses against Copenhagen's extensive defences and trained Danish militia.
segments.body: The fortifications, garrison, and organized Danish militia should have imposed serious costs on an attacker. Instead, the British campaign achieved far more than the speaker considers likely from the initial military balance. The comparison is used to separate a simple story of national superiority from the specific institutional and situational advantages that could make an apparently unlikely operation succeed.

evidence.note: The speaker contrasts the defences and militia with the unexpectedly low British losses and questions a simple exceptionalist explanation.

segments.title: Peymann's impossible fleet decision
segments.summary: Danish commander Henrik Ernst Peymann had to preserve Copenhagen and deny its fleet to Britain, but could achieve neither objective.
segments.body: Peymann's orders were to hold Copenhagen and, if necessary, burn the fleet rather than allow Britain to seize it. The speaker sees understandable reasons not to destroy ships that might still serve as bargaining leverage, especially when surrendering them could spare the city. Yet the episode also exposes unused options: a defended harbour fleet could have been dispersed, better secured, or sent to sea before the British pressure hardened into a blockade and attack.

evidence.note: The transcript names Peymann's duty to hold Copenhagen or burn the fleet and considers the choices he did not take.

segments.title: Evacuation and Copenhagen's civilian fire response
segments.summary: Copenhagen's advanced fire measures depended on civilians who had been allowed to leave before the bombardment.
segments.body: The city possessed comparatively advanced fire-fighting arrangements, but much of the manpower behind them was civilian. Evacuation therefore had a grim double effect: it protected many inhabitants from bombardment while leaving fewer people available to contain the fires. This watch point connects the human dimension of a siege to the vulnerability of a densely built wooden city under incendiary attack.

evidence.note: The transcript explains that civilian manpower was essential to Copenhagen's fire-fighting measures.

segments.title: How the Royal Navy used the captured fleet
segments.summary: The captured Danish fleet was selectively altered, laid up, used, or discarded according to what the Royal Navy actually needed.
segments.body: British naval architects reduced some Danish ships to preferred gun ratings, while other captured vessels were altered to fit Royal Navy requirements. Ships of the line offered a reserve against a longer or more costly war, but many never became regular operational units. Frigates mattered more immediately because the Royal Navy was chronically short of the smaller cruising ships needed for escort, scouting, and overseas duties.

evidence.note: The transcript covers re-rating captured Danish ships, the limited use of ships of the line, and the greater value of frigates.

segments.title: Frigates, small ships, and usable sea power
segments.summary: Frigates made a navy effective beyond battle fleets by scouting, escorting convoys, training officers, and covering routine duties.
segments.body: The speaker calls frigates the eyes of the fleet and distinguishes a battle fleet from the vessels that perform its wider daily work. They escorted convoys, scouted, supported distant commitments, and provided commands in which officers could gain experience before handling larger ships. A comparison with modern fleet totals makes the same point: many smaller vessels can be strategically rational when their missions demand presence and utility rather than only capital-ship tonnage.

evidence.note: The transcript explains frigates' escort, scouting, training, and routine-service functions and compares the logic with modern fleets.

segments.title: The strategic case for seizing the Danish fleet
segments.summary: The discussion frames Britain's strike as an effort to keep the Danish fleet from threatening sea lanes as Denmark faced French pressure.
segments.body: Denmark's interest in the Continental System is presented as a survival calculation between British pressure and the expanding French Empire, not a simple morality tale. Its fleet was valuable leverage in that dilemma, but its possible alignment with France was also exactly what alarmed Britain. The stated strategic purpose of the expedition was therefore not to keep Denmark out of an alliance, but to remove a naval threat and preserve open sea lanes.

evidence.note: The transcript links Danish calculations about the Continental System with Britain's aim of denying the fleet and keeping sea lanes open.

segments.title: Why Copenhagening became a verb
segments.summary: The second Copenhagen operation illustrates a repeated British willingness to use pre-emptive naval force against a fleet left exposed.
segments.body: The name is treated as a shorthand for forceful action repeated around Copenhagen, not merely a label for a single battle. Denmark had concentrated much of its army against the French danger to the south, but its expensive rebuilt fleet remained exposed in Copenhagen. The speaker's practical critique is that the fleet could have been moved to a safer anchorage or put to sea rather than left for Britain to seize.

evidence.note: The speaker explains the repeated Copenhagen precedent and questions why Denmark left its rebuilt fleet insecure.

segments.title: Denmark caught between France and Britain
segments.summary: The closing analysis treats Denmark's response as a constrained attempt to avoid a wider Baltic war while caught between two great powers.
segments.body: The speaker notes that trade between Denmark and Britain appears to have continued despite the crisis, while British evacuation of civilians limited one political cost of the bombardment. He rejects a conspiracy theory but argues that Danish leaders had to think pragmatically about their position between France and Britain. In that reading, the weak relief effort, the fleet's surrender, and Denmark's slow move into the Continental System reflect a state trying to survive an impossible strategic position rather than a coordinated bargain with Britain.

evidence.note: The transcript considers continued trade, civilian evacuation, the weak relief effort, and Denmark's constrained choices between France and Britain.
